title: WHAT - Asking for Information
question_text: How to use WHAT in questions?
content_data: {"formula":"What + verb be + subject? OR What + do/does + subject + main verb?","affirmative":"What is your name?","negative":"What don't you understand?","question":"What do you want?","conjugations":[{"person":"Asking for identity","aff":"What is your name?","ques":"What do you do for work?"},{"person":"Asking about objects","aff":"What is this?","ques":"What colour is your car?"}]}

title: WHAT - Portuguese vs English Difference
question_text: When Portuguese uses "Qual", English uses "What"
content_data: {"usage1":"Names & Identity","example1a":"What is your name? (NOT: Which is your name)","example1b":"What is your phone number?","usage2":"Characteristics","example2":"What colour is your car? What time is it?","teacher_note":"In Portuguese you say 'Qual é seu nome?' but in English we say 'What is your name?' This is a very common mistake to avoid!"}

title: WHO - Asking About People
question_text: How to ask about people using WHO?
content_data: {"formula":"Who + verb + complement?","affirmative":"Who is your teacher?","negative":"Who isn't coming?","question":"Who called you?","conjugations":[{"person":"Identity","aff":"Who are you?","ques":"Who is that woman?"},{"person":"People in actions","aff":"Who did this?","ques":"Who called you?"},{"person":"With prepositions","aff":"Who do you live with?","ques":"Who are you talking to?"}]}

title: WHO - Prepositions at the End
question_text: Why prepositions go at the end of WHO questions
content_data: {"bullets":["In English, prepositions (with, for, to, about) go at the END of WHO questions","❌ With who do you live? (too formal, unnatural)","✅ Who do you live with? (correct, natural)","❌ To who are you talking?","✅ Who are you talking to?","❌ For who is this gift?","✅ Who is this gift for?"],"explanation":"Portuguese puts the preposition at the beginning ('Com quem você mora?'). English puts it at the end. This is one of the biggest differences between the two languages. Don't try to translate word-for-word!"}

title: WHERE - Asking About Places
question_text: How to ask about locations using WHERE?
content_data: {"formula":"Where + be + subject? (location) OR Where + do/does + subject + main verb? (action in a place)","affirmative":"Where is the supermarket?","negative":"Where isn't she?","question":"Where do you live?","conjugations":[{"person":"Location","aff":"Where are you?","ques":"Where is the bathroom?"},{"person":"Origin","aff":"Where are you from?","ques":"Where do you come from?"},{"person":"Action location","aff":"Where does she work?","ques":"Where do you study?"}]}

title: WHERE - The "FROM" Rule
question_text: Using WHERE with "from" to ask about origin
content_data: {"usage1":"Asking where someone was born/raised","example1a":"Where are you from? = De onde você é?","example1b":"Where do you come from? = De onde você vem?","usage2":"The preposition comes at the end","example2":"NOT: From where are you? (too formal)","teacher_note":"Just like Portuguese puts 'de' at the beginning ('De onde você é?'), English puts 'from' at the end. Same meaning, different word order!"}

title: WHEN - Asking About Time
question_text: How to ask about days, dates, hours using WHEN?
content_data: {"formula":"When + be + subject? (fixed event) OR When + do/does + subject + main verb? (action time)","affirmative":"When is your birthday?","negative":"When don't you work?","question":"When does the movie start?","conjugations":[{"person":"Future time","aff":"When is the party?","ques":"When will you arrive?"},{"person":"Past time","aff":"When were you born?","ques":"When did you arrive?"},{"person":"Daily routines","aff":"When do you wake up?","ques":"When do you have lunch?"}]}

title: WHEN - Answering with Time Expressions
question_text: How to answer WHEN questions
content_data: {"usage1":"Specific times","example1a":"At 7 o'clock / at 8:30","example1b":"At noon / at midnight","usage2":"Days and dates","example2":"On Monday / on July 4th / on my birthday","usage3":"Months and years","example3":"In June / in 2024 / in summer","usage4":"Relative time","example4":"Yesterday / tomorrow / next week / two days ago"}

title: WHY - Asking for Reasons
question_text: How to ask for explanations using WHY?
content_data: {"formula":"Why + do/does/did + subject + main verb? OR Why + be + subject + adjective?","affirmative":"Why are you sad?","negative":"Why aren't you coming?","question":"Why do you study English?","conjugations":[{"person":"Emotions","aff":"Why are you happy?","ques":"Why is she crying?"},{"person":"Actions","aff":"Why did you leave?","ques":"Why do you work so hard?"},{"person":"Explanations","aff":"Why is the store closed?","ques":"Why didn't you call?"}]}

title: WHY - Answering with BECAUSE
question_text: How to answer WHY questions correctly
content_data: {"usage1":"Standard answer structure","example1a":"Why are you studying? → Because I have a test tomorrow.","example1b":"Why is she late? → Because the bus didn't come.","usage2":"BECAUSE vs BECAUSE OF","example2":"Because + clause (subject + verb): Because I was tired.","example2b":"Because of + noun: Because of the traffic.","teacher_note":"In Portuguese, 'por que' (question) vs 'porque' (answer). English also has this difference: 'Why' asks, 'Because' answers!"}

title: WHICH - Choosing from Limited Options
question_text: How to ask for a choice from a specific group
content_data: {"formula":"Which + noun + verb? OR Which + verb? (when noun is understood)","affirmative":"Which shirt do you prefer?","negative":"Which one don't you like?","question":"Which is your car?","conjugations":[{"person":"Two options","aff":"Which colour, black or white?","ques":"Which hand do you write with?"},{"person":"Multiple options","aff":"Which of these books is yours?","ques":"Which day works for you?"},{"person":"Singular vs plural","aff":"Which one? / Which ones?","ques":"Which shoes are you wearing?"}]}

title: WHAT vs WHICH - The Critical Difference
question_text: When to use WHAT (open) vs WHICH (limited choices)
content_data: {"bullets":["WHAT = unlimited possibilities. The answer is open.","WHICH = limited options. The answer comes from a small, specific group.","WHAT example: 'What movie do you want to watch?' (any movie in the world)","WHICH example: 'Which movie do you want: Comedy or Horror?' (only two choices)","WHAT: 'What is your phone number?' (unique answer, general info)","WHICH: 'Which number is yours?' (looking at a list of 3 numbers)"],"explanation":"If you can imagine a short list of options (2, 3, or 4 things), use WHICH. If you cannot imagine a short list because there are too many possibilities, use WHAT. This is different from Portuguese where 'qual' is used more often."}

title: WHOSE - Asking About Possession
question_text: How to ask who owns something using WHOSE
content_data: {"formula":"Whose + noun + be + ...?","affirmative":"Whose phone is this?","negative":"Whose car isn't working?","question":"Whose books are these?","conjugations":[{"person":"Single item","aff":"Whose pen is this?","ques":"Whose idea was it?"},{"person":"Multiple items","aff":"Whose keys are these?","ques":"Whose children are they?"},{"person":"Answer format","aff":"It's my pen / It's mine","ques":"It's hers / It's his"}]}

title: WHOSE - Answering with Possessives
question_text: How to answer WHOSE questions using possessive adjectives and pronouns
content_data: {"usage1":"Possessive adjectives (need a noun)","example1a":"It's my pen. / It's your book. / It's his car.","example1b":"It's her phone. / It's our house. / It's their dog.","usage2":"Possessive pronouns (replace the noun)","example2a":"It's mine. / It's yours. / It's his.","example2b":"It's hers. / It's ours. / It's theirs.","teacher_note":"Possessive pronouns are shorter and more natural in conversation. 'Whose phone is this?' → 'Mine.' (one word!)"}

title: HOW - Manner and Condition
question_text: Using HOW to ask about way, state, and condition
content_data: {"formula":"How + be + subject? (condition) OR How + do/does + subject + main verb? (manner)","affirmative":"How are you?","negative":"How isn't it working?","question":"How do you make pizza?","conjugations":[{"person":"Condition/health","aff":"How is your father?","ques":"How are you feeling?"},{"person":"Manner/way","aff":"How does this work?","ques":"How do I get there?"},{"person":"Quality","aff":"How was the movie?","ques":"How did you like it?"}]}

title: HOW OLD - Asking About Age
question_text: Using HOW OLD to ask someone's age
content_data: {"formula":"How old + be + subject?","affirmative":"How old are you?","negative":"How old isn't he?","question":"How old is your brother?","conjugations":[{"person":"Person","aff":"How old is she?","ques":"How old are your parents?"},{"person":"Thing","aff":"How old is this building?","ques":"How old is your car?"},{"person":"Answer format","aff":"I am 25 years old. / I'm 25.","ques":"She's ten (years old)."}]}

title: HOW MANY vs HOW MUCH
question_text: The critical difference between countable and uncountable nouns
content_data: {"formula":"HOW MANY + plural countable noun? | HOW MUCH + uncountable noun?","affirmative":"How many apples? / How much water?","negative":"How many people aren't coming?","question":"How many sisters do you have?","conjugations":[{"person":"Countable (can count individually)","aff":"apples, cars, books, people, ideas","ques":"How many students are in the class?"},{"person":"Uncountable (cannot count individually)","aff":"water, sugar, money, time, information","ques":"How much coffee do you drink?"},{"person":"Price (HOW MUCH + no noun)","aff":"How much is this shirt?","ques":"How much does it cost?"}]}

title: HOW OFTEN - Asking About Frequency
question_text: Using HOW OFTEN to ask how frequently something happens
content_data: {"formula":"How often + do/does + subject + main verb?","affirmative":"How often do you exercise?","negative":"How often don't you sleep well?","question":"How often does she travel?","conjugations":[{"person":"Answer with frequency adverbs","aff":"always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never","ques":"I exercise twice a week."},{"person":"Answer with time expressions","aff":"every day, once a week, three times a month","ques":"She visits her parents every Sunday."},{"person":"Portuguese equivalent","aff":"Com que frequência...?","ques":"Quantas vezes...?"}]}

title: HOW LONG - Asking About Duration
question_text: Using HOW LONG to ask about length of time
content_data: {"formula":"How long + be + subject? (duration of an event) OR How long + does it take + to + verb? (time needed)","affirmative":"How long is the movie?","negative":"How long isn't the flight?","question":"How long does it take to get there?","conjugations":[{"person":"Duration of an event","aff":"How long is the class?","ques":"The movie is two hours long."},{"person":"Time needed for an action","aff":"How long does it take to cook?","ques":"It takes about 30 minutes."},{"person":"Answer format","aff":"for two hours, since Monday, three days","ques":"I've been waiting for an hour."}]}

title: HOW FAR - Asking About Distance
question_text: Using HOW FAR to ask about physical distance
content_data: {"formula":"How far + be + subject + from + place?","affirmative":"How far is your house from here?","negative":"How far isn't the station?","question":"How far do you live from work?","conjugations":[{"person":"Distance in km/miles","aff":"It's 10 kilometers away.","ques":"It's about 5 miles from here."},{"person":"Distance in time","aff":"It's a 20-minute walk.","ques":"It takes 10 minutes by car."},{"person":"Portuguese equivalent","aff":"Qual a distância? / Quão longe?","ques":"Sua casa fica muito longe daqui?"}]}

title: HOW COME - Informal WHY
question_text: Using HOW COME for casual questions about reason
content_data: {"usage1":"Informal replacement for WHY","example1a":"How come you're late? = Why are you late?","example1b":"How come she didn't come? = Why didn't she come?","usage2":"Grammar difference - no auxiliary needed","example2a":"WHY: Why are you sad? (need auxiliary 'are')","example2b":"HOW COME: How come you're sad? (no extra auxiliary, just subject + verb)","teacher_note":"'How come' is very common in everyday speech. It's less formal than 'why' and doesn't follow the normal question word order with auxiliary verbs."}

title: HOW ABOUT - Making Suggestions
question_text: Using HOW ABOUT to offer or suggest something
content_data: {"usage1":"Suggesting an activity","example1a":"How about going to the cinema? = Que tal ir ao cinema?","example1b":"How about we eat out tonight? = E se a gente jantar fora hoje?","usage2":"Offering something","example2a":"How about a coffee? = Que tal um café?","example2b":"How about some help? = Aceita uma ajuda?","usage3":"Form after HOW ABOUT","example3":"How about + noun (How about a break?) OR How about + verb-ing (How about taking a break?)","teacher_note":"Very common in spoken English for polite offers and suggestions. More natural than the formal 'Would you like to...?' in casual situations."}

title: The 5W-1H Method for Projects
question_text: Asking all six questions to understand any situation completely
content_data: {"bullets":["WHAT = the topic, action, or object → What will we sell?","WHY = the reason or goal → Why open a coffee shop?","WHO = the people involved → Who will work there?","WHERE = the place or location → Where will it be?","WHEN = the time or timeline → When will it open?","HOW = the method or process → How will we make coffee?"],"explanation":"In business, school projects, and news reporting, professionals use the 5W-1H method to make sure they understand 100% of a situation. Start with any question, then ask all six. You'll never miss an important detail again!"}

title: The Probability Scale - Your Certainty Compass
question_text: How sure are you? From 0% to 100%
content_data: {"formula":"Modal verbs express different levels of certainty","affirmative":"100% will / 95% must / 80% should / 60% could / 50% may / 30% might / 0% can't","negative":"will not (won't) / must not (mustn't) / should not (shouldn't)","question":"Will it rain? / Could he be right? / May I leave?","conjugations":[{"person":"100% Certain","aff":"will","neg":"won't","ques":"Will it...?"},{"person":"95% Certain (deduction)","aff":"must","neg":"can't","ques":"Must it...?"},{"person":"80% Likely","aff":"should","neg":"shouldn't","ques":"Should it...?"},{"person":"60% Possible","aff":"could","neg":"couldn't","ques":"Could it...?"},{"person":"50% Equal chance","aff":"may","neg":"may not","ques":"May it...?"},{"person":"30% Weak possibility","aff":"might","neg":"might not","ques":"Might it...?"}]}

title: The Three Golden Rules of Modal Verbs
question_text: Three rules with NO exceptions that govern all modal verbs
content_data: {"bullets":["Rule 1 - NO CONJUGATION: Modals never change for he/she/it. ❌ He cans swim. ✅ He can swim.","Rule 2 - BASE FORM AFTER MODAL: No 'to', no -ing, no past tense. ❌ I must to go. ✅ I must go.","Rule 3 - NO 'DO/DOES' IN QUESTIONS/NEGATIVES: Move the modal to the front. ❌ Do you can help? ✅ Can you help?"],"explanation":"Modal verbs are like Lego bricks - they click into place without changing shape. Once you learn these three rules, you never have to worry about -s, -ed, or -ing endings with modals. This makes them easier than regular verbs in many ways!"}

title: CAN - Ability, Permission, and Possibility
question_text: All the ways to use CAN in English
content_data: {"formula":"Subject + can + base verb","affirmative":"I can swim. She can speak French.","negative":"I cannot (can't) swim. She can't drive.","question":"Can you help me? Can I borrow your pen?","conjugations":[{"person":"Ability (saber/conseguir)","aff":"I can play piano","neg":"I can't sing","ques":"Can you cook?"},{"person":"Permission (informal)","aff":"You can go now","neg":"You can't smoke here","ques":"Can I leave early?"},{"person":"Request","ques":"Can you pass the salt?"},{"person":"Offer","ques":"Can I help you with that?"},{"person":"General possibility","aff":"It can get cold here","neg":"That can't be true","ques":"Can this really happen?"}]}

title: CAN - Common Mistakes for Portuguese Speakers
question_text: Avoid these frequent errors when using CAN
content_data: {"usage1":"Never use CAN for future specific guesses","example1a":"❌ It can rain tomorrow. ✅ It may/might/could rain tomorrow.","example1b":"Can is for general truths: 'It can rain a lot in winter' (always true)","usage2":"Never add 'to' after CAN","example2a":"❌ I can to swim. ✅ I can swim.","example2b":"The structure is modal + base verb - no infinitive!","usage3":"Never conjugate CAN","example3":"❌ He cans speak. ✅ He can speak.","teacher_note":"In Portuguese, 'poder' covers many situations. English splits these into CAN, COULD, MAY, MIGHT. Always ask: Am I talking about ability, permission, or possibility?"}

title: COULD - Polite Requests, Past Ability, Possibility
question_text: All the ways to use COULD in English
content_data: {"formula":"Subject + could + base verb","affirmative":"I could run fast when I was young. It could rain later.","negative":"I couldn't hear you. It couldn't be true.","question":"Could you help me? Could I borrow your car?","conjugations":[{"person":"Polite request","ques":"Could you open the window?"},{"person":"Past ability","aff":"When I was 5, I could swim","neg":"I couldn't read until I was 7","ques":"Could you speak English as a child?"},{"person":"Possibility (weaker than can)","aff":"He could be at home","neg":"He couldn't have done it","ques":"Could she be telling the truth?"},{"person":"Suggestion","aff":"You could try restarting your phone","neg":"You couldn't do that","ques":"Could we leave earlier?"},{"person":"Conditional","aff":"If I had money, I could buy a car","neg":"I couldn't go if I wanted to","ques":"Could you do it if you tried?"}]}

title: CAN vs COULD - The Politeness and Probability Difference
question_text: When to choose CAN versus COULD for requests and possibility
content_data: {"bullets":["REQUESTS - Politeness scale: CAN (40% casual) vs COULD (70% polite)","CAN you help me? (to a friend, direct)","COULD you help me? (to a stranger, more polite)","POSSIBILITY - Probability: CAN (80% likely as general truth) vs COULD (60% possible now)","'It can be dangerous here' = general truth (always dangerous)","'It could be dangerous tonight' = specific possibility (might be, might not)","PAST ABILITY: COULD for general past ability","'I could run fast when I was young' = many times, general ability","For one specific past achievement, use 'was able to' or 'managed to'","❌ It was hard but I could finish. ✅ It was hard but I managed to finish."],"explanation":"COULD is the softer, more polite, less certain version of CAN. Use it when you want to be more respectful or when you're less sure about something. In Brazilian Portuguese, 'poderia' is similar in politeness to 'could.'"}

title: MAY - Formal Permission and 50/50 Possibility
question_text: All the ways to use MAY in English
content_data: {"formula":"Subject + may + base verb","affirmative":"I may go to the party. She may be late.","negative":"I may not have time. She may not come.","question":"May I leave early? May I come in?","conjugations":[{"person":"Formal permission","aff":"You may go now","neg":"You may not smoke here","ques":"May I speak to the manager?"},{"person":"Possibility (50/50)","aff":"We may go to the beach","neg":"We may not have enough time","ques":"May it be true?"},{"person":"Wish/hope (fixed expressions)","aff":"May you always be happy"}]}

title: MAY vs MIGHT - The Probability Difference
question_text: Understanding the subtle difference between MAY (50%) and MIGHT (30%)
content_data: {"bullets":["MAY = 50% chance - real possibility, equally likely to happen or not","MIGHT = 30% chance - weaker possibility, less likely to happen","'I may go to the party' = I haven't decided, 50/50 chance","'I might go to the party' = It's possible but unlikely (30%)","In everyday speech, many native speakers use MIGHT for both","But the formal difference is important for exams and precise writing","MAY is also more formal for permission than MIGHT","'May I leave?' (formal, classroom) vs 'Might I suggest...?' (extremely formal, rare)"],"explanation":"Think of a coin flip for MAY (50/50). Think of rolling a die and needing a 6 for MIGHT (about 17% - even less likely). The difference is small but real. In Brazilian Portuguese, both often translate as 'pode ser que' or 'talvez.'"}

title: MIGHT - Weak Possibility and Tentative Suggestions
question_text: All the ways to use MIGHT in English
content_data: {"formula":"Subject + might + base verb","affirmative":"I might come tomorrow. She might be stuck in traffic.","negative":"I might not have time. He might not remember.","question":"Might I suggest an alternative? (very formal)","conjugations":[{"person":"Weak possibility (30%)","aff":"We might go to the cinema","neg":"We might not have enough money","ques":"Might it be true?"},{"person":"Tentative suggestion","aff":"You might want to apologise","neg":"You might not want to say that"},{"person":"Past possibility","aff":"She might have missed the bus","neg":"She might not have seen the message","ques":"Might she have forgotten?"}]}

title: SHOULD - Advice, Expectation, and Regret
question_text: All the ways to use SHOULD in English
content_data: {"formula":"Subject + should + base verb","affirmative":"You should see a doctor. The train should arrive soon.","negative":"You shouldn't eat so much sugar. He shouldn't be late.","question":"Should I call her? Should we wait?","conjugations":[{"person":"Advice / Recommendation","aff":"You should study more","neg":"You shouldn't smoke","ques":"Should I take the job?"},{"person":"Expectation (80% sure)","aff":"He should be home by now","neg":"She shouldn't be long","ques":"Should the bus be here yet?"},{"person":"Past regret (should have)","aff":"I should have studied harder","neg":"I shouldn't have said that","ques":"Should I have told her?"}]}

title: SHOULD vs MUST - Advice vs Obligation
question_text: Understanding the strength difference between SHOULD and MUST
content_data: {"bullets":["SHOULD = recommendation, advice (80% strength) - you have a choice","MUST = obligation, necessity (100% strength) - no choice, it's required","'You should see a doctor' = I recommend it, but you decide","'You must wear a seatbelt' = It's the law, you have no choice","For logical deduction: MUST is 95% certain, SHOULD is 80% certain","'The lights are on - they must be home' (very sure)","'The train should arrive at 5 PM' (based on schedule, but delays possible)","Negative forms: shouldn't (advice against) vs must not (prohibition, forbidden)","'You shouldn't eat late' (advice) vs 'You must not enter' (you're not allowed)"],"explanation":"Think of SHOULD as a strong suggestion and MUST as a command. In Brazilian Portuguese, 'você deveria' matches SHOULD, and 'você tem que' or 'você precisa' matches MUST."}

title: MUST - Obligation and Logical Deduction
question_text: All the ways to use MUST in English
content_data: {"formula":"Subject + must + base verb","affirmative":"You must wear a helmet. She must be tired - she worked all night.","negative":"You must not (mustn't) touch that wire.","question":"Must I finish today? (rare - usually 'Do I have to?')","conjugations":[{"person":"Obligation (law/rule)","aff":"Employees must wear ID","neg":"You must not smoke here","ques":"Must we arrive early?"},{"person":"Logical deduction (95% sure)","aff":"She's crying - she must be sad","neg":"That can't be true - he's in Tokyo","ques":"Must it be this way?"},{"person":"Strong internal need","aff":"I must finish this report","neg":"I must not forget"}]}

title: MUST vs HAVE TO - Obligation from Inside vs Outside
question_text: Understanding where the obligation comes from
content_data: {"bullets":["MUST = internal obligation (I feel I need to do this) OR very strong external rules","HAVE TO = external obligation (someone/something else requires it)","'I must finish this project' = I feel personally that I need to do it","'I have to finish this project' = my boss requires it, the deadline is tomorrow","In everyday speech, HAVE TO is much more common for external obligations","MUST is often too strong for casual conversation","The biggest trap: MUSTN'T vs DON'T HAVE TO","MUSTN'T = it is forbidden (proibido)","DON'T HAVE TO = it is not necessary (não precisa)","❌ You mustn't pay for the ticket (sounds like it's illegal to pay)","✅ You don't have to pay for the ticket (it's free - no need to pay)"],"explanation":"This is one of the most common mistakes for Portuguese speakers! In Portuguese, 'não pode' can mean both 'you must not' (forbidden) and 'you don't have to' (not necessary). English separates these completely. MUSTN'T = forbidden. DON'T HAVE TO = not necessary."}

title: WILL - Certainty, Promises, and Spontaneous Decisions
question_text: All the ways to use WILL in English
content_data: {"formula":"Subject + will + base verb","affirmative":"I will call you tomorrow. It will rain.","negative":"I will not (won't) forget. She won't be late.","question":"Will you help me? Will it be sunny?","conjugations":[{"person":"Future action (100% certain)","aff":"The sun will rise at 6:15","neg":"It won't snow in July","ques":"Will you be there?"},{"person":"Promise","aff":"I will always love you","neg":"I will never forget"},{"person":"Spontaneous decision","aff":"The phone's ringing - I'll get it","neg":"I won't answer"},{"person":"Offer","aff":"I'll carry those bags for you"}]}

title: WILL vs GOING TO - Predicting vs Planning
question_text: Understanding the difference between WILL (prediction) and GOING TO (plan/evidence)
content_data: {"bullets":["WILL = prediction based on opinion, spontaneous decision, promise","GOING TO = plan made before speaking, prediction based on present evidence","'I think it will rain tomorrow' = opinion, guess","'Look at those dark clouds - it's going to rain' = evidence (you see the clouds)","'The phone is ringing - I'll get it' = spontaneous decision (just decided now)","'I'm going to call my mom later' = plan made before (already decided)","Promise: always WILL - 'I will never tell anyone'","For predictions without evidence, both are possible with slight nuance"],"explanation":"This difference is subtle but important. WILL is for instant decisions, promises, and predictions based on what you think. GOING TO is for planned actions and predictions based on what you can see. In Brazilian Portuguese, both often translate to 'vou' which makes this difficult - pay attention to whether there's evidence or a pre-existing plan!"}

title: WOULD - Polite Offers, Habits, and Hypotheticals
question_text: All the ways to use WOULD in English
content_data: {"formula":"Subject + would + base verb","affirmative":"I would like some coffee. When I was young, I would play outside.","negative":"I wouldn't do that if I were you. She wouldn't say that.","question":"Would you like some tea? Would you mind closing the door?","conjugations":[{"person":"Polite offer/request","ques":"Would you like help? / Would you mind waiting?"},{"person":"Past habit","aff":"We would visit grandma every Sunday","neg":"I wouldn't eat vegetables as a child","ques":"Would you play there often?"},{"person":"Unreal conditional","aff":"If I were rich, I would travel","neg":"I wouldn't do that if I were you","ques":"Would you go if you could?"},{"person":"Reported speech","aff":"He said he would come","neg":"She said she wouldn't be late"}]}

title: WOULD for Hypotheticals - The Unreal Conditional
question_text: Using WOULD to talk about imaginary situations
content_data: {"bullets":["Unreal conditional = IF + past simple, WOULD + base verb","The situation is NOT real - it's imaginary or impossible","'If I were rich, I would buy a yacht' (but I'm not rich)","'If I were you, I would apologise' (I can't be you - giving advice)","'What would you do if you won the lottery?' (imagining a future possibility)","Notice: we use WERE for all persons in the IF clause (even I/he/she)","'If I were...' not 'If I was...' (though some native speakers say 'was' in casual speech)","This is different from the first conditional (possible situations):","First conditional: 'If it rains, I will stay home' (possible, 50% chance)","Second conditional (unreal): 'If I won the lottery, I would travel' (unlikely, imaginary)"],"explanation":"WOULD doesn't fit on the probability scale because it describes imaginary or habitual situations, not real predictions about the future. Think of WOULD as the past or imaginary version of WILL."}

title: Permission and Politeness - The Politeness Ladder
question_text: How to ask for permission from casual to ultra-polite
content_data: {"formula":"Politeness level determines which modal to use","affirmative":"Can (40% casual) / Could (60% polite) / May (70% formal) / Would you mind (80% ultra-polite)","negative":"Can't (direct refusal) / Couldn't (polite refusal) / May not (formal refusal)","question":"Can I use your phone? / Could I leave early? / May I speak to the manager?","conjugations":[{"person":"Casual (friends, family)","aff":"Can I borrow £5?","neg":"You can't go","ques":"Can I sit here?"},{"person":"Polite (strangers, colleagues)","aff":"Could I borrow £5?","neg":"You couldn't possibly","ques":"Could I have a moment?"},{"person":"Formal (professional, official)","aff":"May I borrow £5?","neg":"You may not","ques":"May I come in?"},{"person":"Ultra-polite (very respectful)","aff":"Would you mind if I borrowed £5?","ques":"Would you mind if I sat here?"}]}

title: Advice - How Strongly to Recommend
question_text: Choosing between COULD, SHOULD, and MUST for giving advice
content_data: {"usage1":"COULD (40% strength) - gentle option, just a suggestion","example1a":"You could try yoga for back pain","example1b":"We could eat at that new restaurant","usage2":"SHOULD (80% strength) - strong recommendation, expectation","example2a":"You should see a dentist about that tooth","example2b":"You should apologise to her","usage3":"MUST (100% strength) - obligation, no choice, command","example3a":"You must stop smoking - it's killing you","example3b":"You must wear a helmet on this site","teacher_note":"In Brazilian Portuguese, 'você poderia' (could), 'você deveria' (should), and 'você tem que' (must) follow the same strength pattern. Match the strength to the situation!"}

title: Modal Perfect - Talking About the Past
question_text: How to express past possibility, regret, and deduction using modal + have + past participle
content_data: {"formula":"Modal + have + past participle (V3)","affirmative":"I should have studied. She must have left.","negative":"I shouldn't have said that. They couldn't have known.","question":"Should I have called? Could she have forgotten?","conjugations":[{"person":"Regret / Past advice not followed","aff":"I should have studied","neg":"I shouldn't have eaten so much","ques":"Should I have told her?"},{"person":"Past ability not used","aff":"We could have won","neg":"We couldn't have beaten them","ques":"Could they have escaped?"},{"person":"Past weak possibility","aff":"She might have missed the train","neg":"He might not have seen the message","ques":"Might she have taken the wrong bus?"},{"person":"Strong past deduction (95%)","aff":"He must have left early - his car is gone","neg":"They can't have arrived yet - it's too early"}]}

title: SHOULD HAVE - Expressing Regret
question_text: Using SHOULD HAVE to talk about past mistakes and regrets
content_data: {"bullets":["SHOULD HAVE = past advice that wasn't followed = regret","'I should have studied more for the test' (but I didn't - now I regret it)","'She should have called me' (she didn't - I'm upset about it)","SHOULDN'T HAVE = past action that was a bad idea = negative regret","'I shouldn't have eaten that much pizza' (I ate it - now I feel sick)","'You shouldn't have told her my secret' (you told her - now everyone knows)","In Brazilian Portuguese: 'eu deveria ter feito' matches this exactly","Common mistake: using the simple past instead of the past participle","❌ I should have went to the doctor","✅ I should have gone to the doctor","❌ She should have called me","✅ She should have called me (called is correct - it's the past participle)"],"explanation":"The modal perfect is how English expresses possibility, ability, and regret in the past. The structure is always MODAL + HAVE + PAST PARTICIPLE. The past participle never changes - the modal tells you the time and meaning."}

title: COULD HAVE - Lost Opportunities
question_text: Using COULD HAVE to talk about past possibilities that didn't happen
content_data: {"bullets":["COULD HAVE = something was possible in the past, but it didn't happen","'You could have asked me for help' (you didn't ask - but I was available)","'We could have won the game' (we lost - but it was possible to win)","'I could have been a doctor' (I chose a different career - but I had the ability)","COULDN'T HAVE = something was impossible in the past","'She couldn't have stolen the money - she was with me all day' (impossible)","'He couldn't have known about the surprise party' (he didn't know - impossible)","Common in excuses and explanations: 'I could have called, but my phone died'","Different from WOULD HAVE (conditional outcome) and MIGHT HAVE (less certain)"],"explanation":"COULD HAVE is about unrealized potential. The ability existed, but the action didn't happen. In Brazilian Portuguese: 'eu poderia ter feito' (but I didn't)."}

title: Simple Present - When to Use It
question_text: The four main uses of Simple Present tense
content_data: {"usage1":"Habits and Routines (ações habituais)","example1a":"I wake up at 7 AM every day.","example1b":"She drinks coffee every morning.","usage2":"General Truths and Facts (verdades universais)","example2a":"Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius.","example2b":"The Earth revolves around the Sun.","usage3":"Fixed Schedules (horários fixos)","example3a":"The train leaves at 6 PM.","example3b":"The movie starts at 8:30.","usage4":"Narrative Present (resumo de filmes/livros)","example4":"In the final chapter, the detective discovers the truth."}

title: Simple Present - Formation Rules
question_text: How to form affirmative, negative, and question sentences
content_data: {"formula":"Subject + base verb (+ -s for he/she/it)","affirmative":"I work / You work / He works / She works / It works / We work / They work","negative":"I don't work / He doesn't work / They don't work","question":"Do you work? / Does she work? / Do they work?","conjugations":[{"person":"I","aff":"I work","neg":"I don't work","ques":"Do I work?"},{"person":"You","aff":"You work","neg":"You don't work","ques":"Do you work?"},{"person":"He/She/It","aff":"He works / She works / It works","neg":"He doesn't work","ques":"Does he work?"},{"person":"We","aff":"We work","neg":"We don't work","ques":"Do we work?"},{"person":"They","aff":"They work","neg":"They don't work","ques":"Do they work?"}]}

title: Simple Present - Spelling Rules for -s/-es
question_text: How to add -s or -es to verbs for he/she/it
content_data: {"bullets":["Most verbs: just add -s (eat → eats, read → reads)","Verbs ending in -ss, -sh, -ch, -x, -o: add -es (kiss → kisses, wash → washes, watch → watches, fix → fixes, go → goes)","Verbs ending in consonant + -y: change y to i and add -es (study → studies, cry → cries, fly → flies)","Verbs ending in vowel + -y: just add -s (play → plays, enjoy → enjoys, say → says)","Common irregulars: have → has, do → does, be → is"],"explanation":"The third person -s is the only conjugation in Simple Present. Unlike Portuguese, you don't change the verb for each person - only for he/she/it. This makes English simpler!"}

title: Present Continuous - When to Use It
question_text: The five main uses of Present Continuous tense
content_data: {"usage1":"Action happening RIGHT NOW (no momento da fala)","example1a":"I am reading this explanation right now.","example1b":"Look! It is snowing outside.","usage2":"Temporary situation (situação temporária)","example2a":"I am staying with a friend this week.","example2b":"She is reading a great book at the moment.","usage3":"Changing/developing situation (tendências)","example3a":"The cost of living is increasing rapidly.","example3b":"My English is getting better every day.","usage4":"Future arrangement (plano fixo para o futuro)","example4a":"We are meeting the clients at 7:30 tomorrow.","example4b":"I am flying to São Paulo next Monday.","usage5":"Annoyance with 'always' (crítica com 'sempre')","example5":"He is always losing his keys! (irritation)"}

title: Present Continuous - Formation Rules
question_text: How to form affirmative, negative, and question sentences with -ing
content_data: {"formula":"Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing","affirmative":"I am working / You are working / He is working / She is working / It is working / We are working / They are working","negative":"I am not working / He isn't working / They aren't working","question":"Am I working? / Is she working? / Are they working?","conjugations":[{"person":"I","aff":"I am working","neg":"I am not working","ques":"Am I working?"},{"person":"You","aff":"You are working","neg":"You aren't working","ques":"Are you working?"},{"person":"He/She/It","aff":"He is working","neg":"He isn't working","ques":"Is he working?"},{"person":"We","aff":"We are working","neg":"We aren't working","ques":"Are we working?"},{"person":"They","aff":"They are working","neg":"They aren't working","ques":"Are they working?"}]}

title: Present Continuous - Spelling Rules for -ING
question_text: How to add -ing to verbs correctly
content_data: {"bullets":["Most verbs: just add -ing (eat → eating, read → reading)","Verbs ending in silent -e: drop the -e, add -ing (make → making, dance → dancing, write → writing)","Exception: verbs ending in -ee: keep both (see → seeing, agree → agreeing)","CVC (consonant-vowel-consonant) one-syllable: double last consonant (sit → sitting, run → running, stop → stopping)","Verbs ending in -ie: change to -y (lie → lying, die → dying, tie → tying)","Two-syllable verbs: double if stress is on last syllable (begin → beginning, but open → opening)"],"explanation":"The -ing form is also called the present participle. It's used not only in Present Continuous but also after verbs like 'enjoy', 'finish', and 'mind' (I enjoy swimming, I finished reading)."}

title: Simple Present vs Present Continuous - The Critical Difference
question_text: Understanding when to use each tense, especially for Portuguese speakers
content_data: {"bullets":["SIMPLE PRESENT = permanent, habitual, general truth (permanente, habitual, verdade geral)","PRESENT CONTINUOUS = temporary, in progress, happening now (temporário, em progresso, acontecendo agora)","Portuguese uses the same tense for both: 'Eu estudo inglês' (habitual) and 'Eu estudo inglês agora' (in progress)","English forces you to choose: 'I study English' (habitual) vs 'I am studying English now' (in progress)","Compare these pairs:","'I work at a bank' = permanent job (Simple Present)","'I am working on a special project' = temporary (Present Continuous)","'She lives in London' = permanent home (Simple Present)","'She is staying with friends' = temporary (Present Continuous)","'He always loses his keys' = neutral fact (Simple Present)","'He is always losing his keys' = complaint/annoyance (Present Continuous)"],"explanation":"This is the biggest difference between English and Portuguese present tenses! In Portuguese, you use the same tense for both. In English, you MUST choose based on whether something is permanent/habitual (Simple) or temporary/in progress (Continuous)."}

title: Stative Verbs - The Exception to Continuous
question_text: Verbs that describe states, not actions - never use them in continuous form
content_data: {"formula":"Stative verbs = NO -ing form. Use Simple Present instead.","affirmative":"I know the answer. (NOT: I am knowing)","negative":"I don't understand. (NOT: I am not understanding)","question":"Do you believe me? (NOT: Are you believing?)","conjugations":[{"person":"Mental states (thoughts)","aff":"I know / believe / understand / remember / think (opinion)","neg":"I don't remember","ques":"Do you understand?"},{"person":"Emotional states (feelings)","aff":"I love / hate / like / want / need / prefer","neg":"I don't like it","ques":"Do you want some?"},{"person":"Sensory perception","aff":"I see / hear / smell / taste / feel","neg":"I don't see it","ques":"Do you hear that?"},{"person":"Possession","aff":"I have (own) / belong / own / possess","neg":"I don't have it","ques":"Do you have a car?"},{"person":"State of being","aff":"I am / seem / appear / cost / weigh","neg":"It doesn't cost much","ques":"Does it seem right?"}]}

title: Stative Verb Categories - Complete List
question_text: Five categories of stative verbs with detailed examples
content_data: {"usage1":"Mental States (cognition, belief)","example1a":"know, believe, understand, remember, forget, think (opinion)","example1b":"mean, suppose, realize, recognize, doubt","usage2":"Emotional States (feelings, desire)","example2a":"like, love, hate, want, need, prefer, wish, desire","example2b":"appreciate, care, envy, fear, forgive, respect","usage3":"Sensory Perception (involuntary senses)","example3a":"see, hear, smell, taste, feel (involuntary)","example3b":"'I see a bird' vs 'I am seeing the doctor' (different meaning!)","usage4":"Possession and Relationship","example4a":"have (possess), own, possess, belong, include, contain","example4b":"consist of, depend on, concern, involve","usage5":"State of Being and Appearance","example5a":"be, seem, appear, look (appearance), sound, resemble","example5b":"cost, weigh, measure, equal"}

title: Dynamic Verbs - The Exceptions (Two Meanings)
question_text: Verbs that can be stative OR dynamic depending on meaning
content_data: {"bullets":["THINK - stative (opinion): 'I think it's a good idea' (NO -ing)","THINK - dynamic (mental process): 'I am thinking about the question' (-ing OK)","HAVE - stative (possess): 'She has a car' (NO -ing)","HAVE - dynamic (experience/consume): 'She is having lunch / a shower' (-ing OK)","SEE - stative (sight): 'I see a rainbow' (NO -ing)","SEE - dynamic (meet): 'I am seeing the doctor tomorrow' (-ing OK)","TASTE - stative (flavor): 'This milk tastes sour' (NO -ing)","TASTE - dynamic (sample): 'The chef is tasting the sauce' (-ing OK)","SMELL - stative (odor): 'The flowers smell lovely' (NO -ing)","SMELL - dynamic (sniff): 'The dog is smelling the ground' (-ing OK)","FEEL - stative (texture): 'Velvet feels soft' (NO -ing)","FEEL - dynamic (touch/emotion): 'I am feeling the fabric / much better' (-ing OK)"],"explanation":"These verbs are tricky because their meaning changes. When they describe a state (opinion, possession, involuntary perception), use Simple Present. When they describe an action (mental process, experience, voluntary action), you can use Present Continuous."}

title: The Verb ENJOY - Special Case
question_text: Why ENJOY is different from other emotion verbs
content_data: {"usage1":"Although ENJOY describes a feeling, it is DYNAMIC","example1a":"I enjoy playing tennis. (Simple Present for general fact)","example1b":"I am enjoying this party very much. (Present Continuous for current experience)","usage2":"Compare with other emotion verbs","example2a":"I love this song. (stative - NO continuous: ❌ I am loving)","example2b":"I am enjoying this song. (dynamic - continuous OK)","teacher_note":"Because 'enjoy' implies ACTIVE, conscious appreciation of an ongoing experience, it works in the continuous form. 'Love' and 'like' are passive states - they don't take continuous."}

title: Special "To Be" - Permanent vs Temporary Behavior
question_text: The only exception: using "being" to show temporary, deliberate behavior
content_data: {"bullets":["To Be is normally stative (never continuous): 'She is a doctor' (NOT: She is being a doctor)","ONE EXCEPTION: am/is/are + being + adjective = temporary BEHAVIOR (not permanent characteristic)","'He is rude' = permanent personality - he is always rude","'He is being rude' = temporary behavior - he is acting rude NOW (maybe not usually)","WHEN TO USE: only for CONTROLLABLE BEHAVIORS (actions the person chooses)","Permissible adjectives: nice, helpful, patient, silly, rude, difficult, lazy, noisy, quiet","NOT permissible (permanent qualities): tall, short, fat, thin, beautiful, old, young, intelligent, stupid","❌ He is being tall (height is not a behavior you can choose)","❌ The car is being red (color is a state, not behavior)","Examples: 'Why are you being so quiet tonight?' (unusual behavior)","'Stop it - you are being silly!' (acting silly now)"],"explanation":"This is an advanced nuance that separates intermediate from advanced speakers. It signals that you understand that BEING describes temporary, chosen behavior, not permanent identity. Very useful for workplace and social situations!"}

title: Simple Past - Regular Verbs (Rule 1: Add -D)
question_text: Verbs ending in silent -e: just add -D
content_data: {"formula":"Base verb + -d (for verbs ending in -e)","affirmative":"love → loved / like → liked / dance → danced","negative":"did not love / didn't like / didn't dance","question":"Did you love it? / Did she like it? / Did they dance?","conjugations":[{"person":"love","aff":"loved","neg":"didn't love","ques":"Did you love?"},{"person":"like","aff":"liked","neg":"didn't like","ques":"Did she like?"},{"person":"dance","aff":"danced","neg":"didn't dance","ques":"Did they dance?"}]}

title: Simple Past - Regular Verbs (Rule 2: Add -ED)
question_text: Most verbs: just add -ED
content_data: {"formula":"Base verb + -ed","affirmative":"work → worked / need → needed / help → helped","negative":"did not work / didn't need / didn't help","question":"Did you work? / Did she need? / Did they help?","conjugations":[{"person":"work","aff":"worked","neg":"didn't work","ques":"Did you work?"},{"person":"need","aff":"needed","neg":"didn't need","ques":"Did she need?"},{"person":"help","aff":"helped","neg":"didn't help","ques":"Did they help?"}]}

title: Simple Past - Spelling Rule: Consonant + Y → -IED
question_text: Change Y to I and add -ED for verbs ending in consonant + Y
content_data: {"bullets":["Rule: consonant + Y → change Y to I, add -ED","study → studied (NOT studyed)","cry → cried (NOT cryed)","try → tried (NOT tryed)","reply → replied","If the Y comes after a VOWEL, just add -ED (no change):","play → played, enjoy → enjoyed, stay → stayed","This is exactly like Portuguese: estudar → estudou (the Y becomes I)","Common mistake: 'I tryed my best' ❌ → 'I tried my best' ✅"],"explanation":"English spelling changes to maintain pronunciation. When a verb ends in consonant + Y, the Y becomes I to keep the vowel sound consistent. Verbs ending in vowel + Y keep the Y because the vowel sound is already clear."}

title: Simple Past - CVC Rule (Double Consonant)
question_text: One-syllable CVC verbs double the final consonant before adding -ED
content_data: {"bullets":["CVC = Consonant - Vowel - Consonant pattern","stop → stopped (double the P)","plan → planned (double the N)","rub → rubbed (double the B)","Do NOT double if the verb ends in W, X, or Y:","snow → snowed (W = no double)","fix → fixed (X = no double)","play → played (Y = no double)","For two-syllable verbs: double only if the LAST syllable is stressed","preFER → preferred (stress on second syllable)","ocCUR → occurred (stress on second syllable)","LISten → listened (stress on first syllable → no double)","TRAvel → traveled (US) / travelled (UK)","Common mistake: 'He stoped the car' ❌ → 'He stopped the car' ✅"],"explanation":"We double the consonant to keep the vowel sound short. Without doubling, 'stopped' would rhyme with 'hoped' - completely different pronunciation. This rule protects the original vowel sound."}

title: Pronunciation of -ED (3 Sounds)
question_text: How to pronounce the -ED ending correctly - /t/, /d/, or /ɪd/
content_data: {"usage1":"/t/ sound - after unvoiced consonants (p, k, f, s, sh, ch, x)","example1a":"stopped (stopT), walked (walkT), kissed (kissT)","example1b":"washed (washT), fixed (fixT)","usage2":"/d/ sound - after vowels and voiced consonants (b, g, l, m, n, r, v, z)","example2a":"played (playD), hugged (hugD), called (callD)","example2b":"loved (loveD), stayed (stayD), closed (closeD)","usage3":"/ɪd/ sound - after T or D only (adds an extra syllable)","example3a":"wanted (wan-ted), needed (nee-ded), hated (ha-ted)","example3b":"waited (wai-ted), decided (de-ci-ded)","teacher_note":"NEVER pronounce -ED as a separate 'ed' syllable unless the verb ends in T or D. 'Worked' is one syllable (workT), not two (work-ed). This is very different from Portuguese!"}

title: Past Continuous - Formation and Uses
question_text: Use was/were + -ing for actions in progress at a past time
content_data: {"formula":"Subject + was/were + verb-ing","affirmative":"I was working / You were working / He was working / We were working / They were working","negative":"I wasn't working / You weren't working / He wasn't working","question":"Was I working? / Were you working? / Was she working?","conjugations":[{"person":"Action at specific past time","aff":"I was having dinner at 7 PM","neg":"I wasn't sleeping","ques":"Were you working at 5?"},{"person":"Interrupted action (long + short)","aff":"I was watching TV when the phone rang","neg":"I wasn't listening when you called","ques":"What were you doing when it happened?"},{"person":"Simultaneous actions","aff":"While I was cooking, he was cleaning","neg":"Neither was working","ques":"Were they talking while you were studying?"},{"person":"Setting the scene","aff":"The sun was shining. Birds were singing","neg":"Nobody was paying attention","ques":"Was anyone listening?"},{"person":"Polite request (fixed)","aff":"I was wondering if you could help"}]}

title: Past Continuous - Signal Words
question_text: Words that tell you to use Past Continuous
content_data: {"usage1":"WHILE (enquanto) - introduces the longer action","example1a":"While I was walking home, I saw an accident.","example1b":"While we were talking, the teacher arrived.","usage2":"WHEN (quando) - usually followed by Simple Past (the interruption)","example2a":"I was sleeping when the phone rang.","example2b":"She was cooking when the fire alarm went off.","usage3":"AT + specific time + YESTERDAY","example3a":"At 5 PM yesterday, I was driving home.","example3b":"What were you doing at midnight?","usage4":"ALL MORNING / ALL DAY / THE WHOLE EVENING","example4a":"I was working all morning.","example4b":"They were traveling all day."}

title: Top 20 Irregular Verbs - Part 1
question_text: Memorize these three forms: Base - Simple Past - Past Participle
content_data: {"formula":"Irregular verbs do NOT follow the -ed rule","affirmative":"be (am/is/are) → was/were → been","negative":"have → had → had / do → did → done","question":"say → said → said / go → went → gone","conjugations":[{"person":"be (ser/estar)","aff":"am/is/are → was/were → been","neg":"am not → wasn't/weren't → haven't been","ques":"Are you? → Were you? → Have you been?"},{"person":"have (ter)","aff":"have/has → had → had","neg":"don't have → didn't have → haven't had","ques":"Do you have? → Did you have? → Have you had?"},{"person":"do (fazer)","aff":"do/does → did → done","neg":"don't/doesn't → didn't → haven't done","ques":"Do/Does → Did → Have you done?"},{"person":"say (dizer)","aff":"say/says → said → said","neg":"don't say → didn't say → haven't said","ques":"Do you say? → Did you say? → Have you said?"},{"person":"go (ir)","aff":"go/goes → went → gone","neg":"don't go → didn't go → haven't gone","ques":"Do you go? → Did you go? → Have you gone?"}]}

title: Top 20 Irregular Verbs - Part 2
question_text: More essential irregular verbs for daily communication
content_data: {"formula":"Grouped by pattern to help memorization","affirmative":"get → got → gotten (US) / got (UK)","negative":"make → made → made / know → knew → known","question":"think → thought → thought / take → took → taken","conjugations":[{"person":"get (obter/pegar)","aff":"get → got → gotten","neg":"don't get → didn't get → haven't gotten","ques":"Do you get? → Did you get? → Have you gotten?"},{"person":"make (fazer)","aff":"make → made → made","neg":"don't make → didn't make → haven't made","ques":"Do you make? → Did you make? → Have you made?"},{"person":"know (saber/conhecer)","aff":"know → knew → known","neg":"don't know → didn't know → haven't known","ques":"Do you know? → Did you know? → Have you known?"},{"person":"think (pensar)","aff":"think → thought → thought","neg":"don't think → didn't think → haven't thought","ques":"Do you think? → Did you think? → Have you thought?"},{"person":"take (pegar/levar)","aff":"take → took → taken","neg":"don't take → didn't take → haven't taken","ques":"Do you take? → Did you take? → Have you taken?"}]}

title: Irregular Verb Patterns - i-a-u
question_text: Grouping irregular verbs by pattern makes memorization easier
content_data: {"bullets":["PATTERN 1: i → a → u (change the vowel)","begin → began → begun","drink → drank → drunk","sing → sang → sung","swim → swam → swum","ring → rang → rung","PATTERN 2: Past Participle ends in -EN","speak → spoke → spoken","break → broke → broken","eat → ate → eaten","drive → drove → driven","write → wrote → written","PATTERN 3: All three forms the same","cut → cut → cut","put → put → put","hit → hit → hit","let → let → let","PATTERN 4: Past and Participle the same (vowel change)","keep → kept → kept","sleep → slept → slept","feel → felt → felt","tell → told → told","sell → sold → sold"],"explanation":"Instead of memorizing 100 irregular verbs randomly, group them by these patterns. You'll learn much faster! Focus on the 20% most common verbs (like these) - they cover 80% of daily usage."}

title: LIE vs LAY - The Most Confusing Verb Pair
question_text: Understand the difference between reclining (lie) and putting something down (lay)
content_data: {"bullets":["LIE = to recline, to rest (deitar-se) - NO OBJECT needed","Past: lay / Past Participle: lain","'Yesterday I LAY on the sofa for two hours' (past of lie)","'I have LAIN here all morning' (present perfect of lie)","LAY = to put something down (colocar) - NEEDS AN OBJECT","Past: laid / Past Participle: laid","'She LAID the book on the table' (object = book)","'He has LAID his keys there before' (object = keys)","LIE (mentir) = to tell a falsehood - regular verb","Past: lied / Past Participle: lied","'He LIED to me' (past of lie - not truth)","MEMORIZATION TRICK:","Lie (recline) = no object → you LIE DOWN (yourself)","Lay (put) = needs an object → you LAY something DOWN","Common mistake: 'I LAID on the bed' ❌ (that means you put something on the bed)","Correct: 'I LAY on the bed' ✅ (past of lie - recline)"],"explanation":"Even native speakers confuse these! The key: if you can replace the verb with 'put,' use LAY (laid/laid). If you can replace it with 'recline' or 'rest,' use LIE (lay/lain). Practice the pairs every day."}

title: HANG - Two Meanings, Two Past Forms
question_text: Use HUNG for objects, HANGED for executions
content_data: {"usage1":"HUNG (for suspending objects - pendurar objetos)","example1a":"I hung the picture on the wall.","example1b":"She hung her coat on the hook.","example1c":"The clothes were hung outside to dry.","usage2":"HANGED (for execution by hanging - enforcar)","example2a":"The prisoner was hanged at dawn.","example2b":"They hanged the criminal in the old Western movie.","teacher_note":"This is one of the few English verbs where the meaning changes the past form. For objects (pictures, clothes, decorations), always use HUNG. For execution of a person, use HANGED. Never use 'hanged' for objects - it sounds very strange!"}

title: SNEAK - Sneaked vs Snuck
question_text: Traditional vs informal past forms of SNEAK
content_data: {"usage1":"SNEAKED (traditional, preferred in formal writing)","example1a":"He sneaked into the room while nobody was watching.","example1b":"She sneaked a piece of cake when her mom wasn't looking.","usage2":"SNUCK (American informal, common in speech)","example2a":"He snuck out the back door.","example2b":"She snuck a peek at the answers.","teacher_note":"In formal writing (essays, business emails, academic work), use SNEAKED. In casual conversation with friends, SNUCK is widely accepted in American English. British English tends to stick with SNEAKED. For A2-B1 level, using SNEAKED is always safe."}

title: SHINE - With or Without Object
question_text: Use SHONE for the sun shining, SHINED for polishing
content_data: {"usage1":"SHONE (no object - intransitive)","example1a":"The sun shone all day. (no object - just the sun shining)","example1b":"The stars shone brightly in the sky.","usage2":"SHINED (with object - transitive)","example2a":"He shined his shoes before the interview.","example2b":"She shined a flashlight under the bed.","teacher_note":"If the sun or stars are doing the shining, use SHONE. If someone is actively polishing an object (shoes, silver) or directing a light (flashlight), use SHINED. Many native speakers use 'shined' for the flashlight and 'shone' only for the sun."}

title: DIVE - Dived vs Dove
question_text: American English uses DOVE, British English uses DIVED
content_data: {"usage1":"DOVE (American English simple past)","example1a":"She dove into the pool.","example1b":"He dove behind the sofa to hide.","usage2":"DIVED (British English, also used in formal US writing)","example2a":"She dived into the water.","example2b":"The submarine dived to avoid detection.","teacher_note":"The past participle is always DIVED in both varieties: 'She has dived in this lake many times.' DOVE is only for simple past. For B1 level, either is acceptable, but be consistent. If you're writing for a British audience, use DIVED. For American, DOVE is fine in conversation."}

title: TO vs FOR - Direction vs Benefit
question_text: TO shows movement/transfer, FOR shows benefit/purpose/duration
content_data: {"formula":"TO = direction, transfer, receiver / FOR = benefit, duration, reason","affirmative":"I go TO school. This gift is FOR you.","negative":"I don't talk TO strangers. This isn't FOR you.","question":"Where did you go? Who is this FOR?","conjugations":[{"person":"Direction (para - movimento)","aff":"I'm going TO the office","neg":"Don't go TO that place","ques":"Where are you going TO?"},{"person":"Benefit (para - benefício)","aff":"This is FOR you","neg":"It's not FOR sale","ques":"Who is this FOR?"},{"person":"Duration (por - tempo)","aff":"I waited FOR two hours","neg":"Not FOR long","ques":"How long did you wait FOR?"},{"person":"Transfer (para - recebimento)","aff":"Give this TO Maria","neg":"Don't send it TO her","ques":"Who should I give it TO?"},{"person":"Reason (por causa de)","aff":"Known FOR its flowers","neg":"Not famous FOR that","ques":"What is it known FOR?"}]}

title: TO vs FOR - Common Mistakes
question_text: Avoid these frequent errors when choosing between TO and FOR
content_data: {"bullets":["EXPLAIN - always TO, never FOR","❌ I explained the rules FOR him","✅ I explained the rules TO him (transfer of information = TO)","THANK - use THANKS FOR (agradecer por), never THANKS TO for thanking someone","❌ Thanks TO your help","✅ Thanks FOR your help (THANKS TO = graças a, different meaning)","WAIT - always WAIT FOR (esperar por), never WAIT TO","❌ I'm waiting TO the bus","✅ I'm waiting FOR the bus","LISTEN - always LISTEN TO (ouvir), never LISTEN FOR","❌ Listen FOR the music","✅ Listen TO the music","USE THE TRICK: If you can replace with 'give' (entregar), use TO","If you can replace with 'for the benefit of' (para o bem de), use FOR"],"explanation":"Portuguese uses 'para' for both direction and benefit, which causes confusion. Think: TO = movement (I go to the store). FOR = benefit (I bought this for you). Also, many verbs have fixed prepositions - you just have to memorize them (wait FOR, listen TO, explain TO)."}

title: MUCH vs MANY - Countable vs Uncountable
question_text: Use MANY for things you can count, MUCH for things you cannot
content_data: {"formula":"MANY + plural countable noun / MUCH + uncountable noun","affirmative":"I have many friends / I have much time (rare - sounds formal)","negative":"I don't have many friends / I don't have much time","question":"How many apples? / How much water?","conjugations":[{"person":"Countable (pode contar)","aff":"apples, cars, books, people","neg":"not many apples","ques":"How many?"},{"person":"Uncountable (não pode contar)","aff":"water, sugar, money, time","neg":"not much water","ques":"How much?"}]}

title: A LOT OF - The Easy Substitute
question_text: Use A LOT OF for everything in affirmative sentences
content_data: {"usage1":"A LOT OF works with BOTH countable and uncountable nouns","example1a":"I have a lot of friends. (countable)","example1b":"I have a lot of money. (uncountable)","usage2":"Use A LOT OF in affirmative sentences (MUCH/MANY sound strange in affirmatives)","example2a":"✅ I have a lot of time.","example2b":"❌ I have much time. (too formal, sounds strange)","usage3":"For negatives and questions, MUCH/MANY are fine","example3a":"I don't have much time. (negative - OK)","example3b":"Do you have many friends? (question - OK)","teacher_note":"In Brazilian Portuguese, 'muito' works for both. In English, if you're not sure, use A LOT OF - it's always safe, especially in affirmative sentences. It sounds natural and native speakers use it constantly."}

title: LOOK FOR - To Search
question_text: Use LOOK FOR when you are searching for something lost
content_data: {"formula":"look + FOR + object (to search, procurar)","affirmative":"I am looking for my phone.","negative":"I'm not looking for anything.","question":"What are you looking for?","conjugations":[{"person":"Present","aff":"I look for my keys every morning","neg":"I'm not looking for trouble","ques":"Are you looking for something?"},{"person":"Past","aff":"I looked for you everywhere","neg":"I didn't look for it","ques":"Did you look for the answer?"},{"person":"Future","aff":"I will look for a new job","neg":"I won't look for it","ques":"Will you look for me?"}]}

title: LOOK FORWARD TO - To Be Excited About
question_text: CRITICAL RULE: After LOOK FORWARD TO, use NOUN or VERB-ING, never infinitive
content_data: {"bullets":["LOOK FORWARD TO = estar ansioso para / esperar com alegria","The 'TO' here is a PREPOSITION, not part of the infinitive","After a preposition, use a NOUN or VERB-ING (gerund)","❌ I look forward to SEE you (wrong - infinitive)","✅ I look forward to SEEING you (correct - -ING form)","✅ I look forward to THE WEEKEND (noun - correct)","Common mistake #1: using FOR instead of TO","❌ She looks forward FOR the party","✅ She looks forward TO the party","Common mistake #2: using infinitive after TO","❌ We look forward to MEET our new boss","✅ We look forward to MEETING our new boss","Common mistake #3: forgetting the -ING","❌ I look forward to see you","✅ I look forward to seeing you","MEMORIZE: TO + -ING with this phrasal verb! It's a fixed rule."],"explanation":"This is one of the most common mistakes at B1 level. In Portuguese, after 'para' you use the infinitive (para VER). In English, after this special 'to,' you use the -ING form. It feels wrong at first, but practice makes it natural. Say it every day: 'I look forward to meeting you!'"}

title: BECAUSE vs SO - Opposite Directions
question_text: BECAUSE introduces the reason, SO introduces the result
content_data: {"formula":"BECAUSE = reason (porque) / SO = result (então/por isso)","affirmative":"I'm tired because I worked late. / I worked late, so I'm tired.","negative":"I'm not happy because I failed. / I failed, so I'm not happy.","question":"Why are you tired? / Did you work late, so you're tired?","conjugations":[{"person":"BECAUSE (reason first or last)","aff":"Result + because + reason","neg":"Because + reason + , + result","ques":"Why? (asks for reason)"},{"person":"SO (result second)","aff":"Reason + , so + result","neg":"Reason + , so not + result","ques":"Did X happen, so Y happened?"}]}

title: BECAUSE vs SO - Never Use Together
question_text: Critical rule: NEVER use BECAUSE and SO in the same sentence
content_data: {"bullets":["English allows only ONE connector per cause-effect relationship","❌ Because I was tired, so I went to bed (DOUBLE CONNECTOR - WRONG)","✅ Because I was tired, I went to bed (reason first, result second)","✅ I was tired, so I went to bed (reason first, result second with SO)","✅ I went to bed because I was tired (result first, reason second)","Think of it like a bridge - you only need one bridge to cross the river!","The same rule applies to other cause-effect words:","❌ Since it was raining, therefore we stayed inside","✅ Since it was raining, we stayed inside","✅ It was raining, therefore we stayed inside","Portuguese often uses double connectors ('porque... então'), but English NEVER does."],"explanation":"This is a very common Portuguese-to-English interference error. In Portuguese, you can say 'Porque estava cansado, então fui dormir.' In English, you MUST choose ONE connector. Either use BECAUSE (no SO) or use SO (no BECAUSE). Never both!"}

title: THEREFORE and AS A RESULT - Formal Connectors
question_text: More formal alternatives to SO for academic and business writing
content_data: {"usage1":"THEREFORE (portanto, consequentemente) - logical conclusion","example1a":"The company lost money. Therefore, it closed.","example1b":"She studied all night; therefore, she passed the exam.","usage2":"AS A RESULT (como resultado) - emphasizes consequence","example2a":"The storm damaged the crops. As a result, prices rose.","example2b":"He didn't train; as a result, he lost the race.","usage3":"Punctuation rules for formal connectors","example3a":"Use a period before: ... . Therefore, ...","example3b":"Use a semicolon before: ... ; therefore, ...","teacher_note":"In spoken English, SO is more natural. In academic essays, business reports, and formal presentations, use THEREFORE or AS A RESULT. They sound more professional and sophisticated."}

title: WITHOUT + Noun/-ING - Expressing Negative Condition
question_text: Use WITHOUT to say 'If X doesn't happen, Y is the result'
content_data: {"usage1":"WITHOUT + noun (sem + substantivo)","example1a":"Without water, plants die. = If there is no water, plants die.","example1b":"Without a visa, you cannot enter the country.","usage2":"WITHOUT + verb-ing (sem + verbo no gerúndio)","example2a":"Without studying, you won't pass the exam.","example2b":"Without adding noise back, the image becomes blurry.","usage3":"WITHOUT is very common in technical explanations","example3a":"Without proper conditioning, the AI ignores the prompt.","example3b":"Without subtracting the generic path, we can't isolate the idea.","teacher_note":"In Portuguese, you would say 'sem + substantivo' or 'sem + verbo no infinitivo' (sem estudar). In English, after WITHOUT, if you use a verb, it must be in the -ING form. 'Without to study' is wrong."}

title: WHEN + Present Simple - Zero Conditional
question_text: Use WHEN + present simple to express general truths and automatic results
content_data: {"formula":"When + present simple clause + , + present simple clause","affirmative":"When you heat ice, it melts.","negative":"When you don't water plants, they die.","question":"What happens when you mix blue and yellow?","conjugations":[{"person":"Scientific fact","aff":"When water reaches 100°C, it boils","neg":"When you don't add yeast, bread doesn't rise","ques":"What happens when...?"},{"person":"System rule","aff":"When you type a prompt, the AI creates an image","neg":"When you don't condition properly, the model ignores you","ques":"What does the system do when...?"},{"person":"Habitual result","aff":"When I'm tired, I drink coffee","neg":"When I'm not hungry, I don't eat","ques":"What do you do when...?"}]}

title: Zero Conditional - Never Use WILL
question_text: Critical rule: Zero Conditional uses present simple in BOTH clauses
content_data: {"bullets":["Zero Conditional = If/When + present simple, present simple","It expresses things that are ALWAYS true (general truths, scientific facts, system rules)","❌ When you heat ice, it WILL melt (wrong - sounds like first conditional)","✅ When you heat ice, it melts (correct - always true)","❌ If you don't study, you WILL fail (first conditional - possible future)","✅ If you don't study, you fail (zero conditional - general truth about the system)","First conditional (possible future): If it rains tomorrow, I WILL stay home","Zero conditional (always true): If it rains, the ground GETS wet","Use Zero Conditional when the result is 100% certain and always happens","Use First Conditional (WILL) when the result is a future prediction (not guaranteed)"],"explanation":"Don't use WILL in Zero Conditional! The present simple in both clauses shows that this is a general truth, not a future prediction. If you use WILL, you change the meaning to a possible future event (First Conditional)."}

title: Basic Sequence Markers - Ordering Steps
question_text: Use these words to show the order of steps in a process
content_data: {"formula":"First, ... Then, ... After that, ... Next, ... Finally,","affirmative":"First, open the app. Then, type your prompt.","negative":"First, don't panic. Then, call for help.","question":"First, what do you do? Then what? Finally?","conjugations":[{"person":"Step 1","aff":"First, you turn on the computer","neg":"First, don't press any buttons","ques":"What do you do first?"},{"person":"Step 2","aff":"Then, you open the browser","neg":"Then, don't close the window","ques":"What do you do then?"},{"person":"Step 3","aff":"After that, you type the URL","neg":"After that, don't forget to save","ques":"What happens after that?"},{"person":"Step 4","aff":"Next, you press enter","neg":"Next, don't click the wrong link","ques":"What's next?"},{"person":"Final step","aff":"Finally, you wait for the page to load","neg":"Finally, don't close too early","ques":"What happens finally?"}]}

title: Time Clauses - BEFORE and AFTER
question_text: Use BEFORE and AFTER to show which action happens first
content_data: {"formula":"BEFORE = later action comes second / AFTER = earlier action comes first","affirmative":"Eat before you go. / After eating, wash your hands.","negative":"Don't call before 9 AM. / Don't text after 10 PM.","question":"What should I do before leaving? / What happens after that?","conjugations":[{"person":"BEFORE (action after BEFORE happens LATER)","aff":"Finish homework BEFORE you watch TV","neg":"Don't leave BEFORE I arrive","ques":"What comes BEFORE the final step?"},{"person":"AFTER (action after AFTER happens EARLIER)","aff":"AFTER you finish, call me","neg":"Don't worry AFTER it's done","ques":"What happens AFTER the first step?"},{"person":"WITH -ING (same subject)","aff":"Before leaving, turn off lights","neg":"Without finishing, you can't leave","ques":"Before starting, what should I know?"}]}

title: Time Clauses - WHILE, AS, and DURING
question_text: Use WHILE/AS for simultaneous actions, DURING for nouns
content_data: {"usage1":"WHILE + clause (two actions happening at the same time)","example1a":"While I was cooking, he was cleaning.","example1b":"While the model runs, the temperature increases.","usage2":"AS (similar to WHILE, often with continuous change)","example2a":"As the sun set, the temperature dropped.","example2b":"As time passes, we learn more.","usage3":"DURING + NOUN (NOT a clause - cannot have a verb)","example3a":"During the movie, I fell asleep. (NOT: During I watched)","example3b":"During training, the AI sees millions of images.","CRITICAL RULE: DURING + NOUN / WHILE + CLAUSE","❌ During I was working (WRONG - clause after DURING)","✅ While I was working (CORRECT)","✅ During work (CORRECT - noun)"}

title: Born in the USA - The Misunderstood Anthem
question_text: Why Springsteen's song is actually a protest against the Vietnam War
content_data: {"bullets":["The song was originally titled 'Vietnam'","Inspired by Ron Kovic, a paralyzed Vietnam War veteran","The CHORUS sounds patriotic, but the VERSES tell a tragic story","Lyrics describe: working-class poverty, conscription, dehumanization of the enemy, broken homecoming, bureaucratic rejection, psychological limbo","Conservative politicians (including Reagan) co-opted the song - Springsteen rejected this publicly","The famous drum sound was created by ACCIDENT (broken reverb plate)","True patriotism = holding your country accountable, not blind loyalty"],"explanation":"This song teaches that you can be fiercely critical of your country's failures while simultaneously being proud of its core ideals. Springsteen's model of 'accountable patriotism' offers a framework for critical citizenship that remains relevant today."}

title: Learning to Fly - Pink Floyd's Metaphor for Fear
question_text: How David Gilmour used flying lessons as a metaphor for leading the band after Roger Waters left
content_data: {"bullets":["Context: 1985, Roger Waters left and declared Pink Floyd 'dead'","Waters sued to stop them from using the name - 2 years of legal battles","Gilmour (quiet guitarist) became unexpected leader - terrified of flying","Took flying lessons to confront his fear - became perfect metaphor","Song structure mirrors overcoming fear: slow, uncertain verses → open, rising chorus","Key line: 'Tongue-tied and twisted, just an earthbound misfit' - honest confession of feeling inadequate","'Ice is forming on my wings' = accumulating pressure (critics, legal battles, self-doubt)","'No navigator to find my way home' = no Roger Waters to guide him","Lesson: You don't need to be ready to begin. You just need to be determined to try."],"explanation":"The song is not about aviation - it's about courage. Gilmour teaches that you don't need to be a natural leader to lead. You only need to be determined to try - even when ice is forming on your wings."}

title: Presentation Skills - Opening and Welcoming
question_text: Essential phrases to start a presentation professionally
content_data: {"usage1":"Formal openings","example1a":"Good morning/afternoon everyone and welcome.","example1b":"First, let me thank you all for being here today.","usage2":"Warm openings","example2a":"It's a pleasure to see you all today.","example2b":"Thank you for being here. / Thank you for coming.","teacher_note":"In Portuguese, we say 'Gostaria de agradecer a presença de todos.' In English, avoid 'thank for the presence' - use 'thank you for being here' instead. The natural structures are different!"}

title: Presentation Skills - The Hook
question_text: Techniques to grab audience attention in the first 30 seconds
content_data: {"usage1":"The Question Hook","example1a":"To begin, I'd like to pose a question...","example1b":"How many of you have ever...?","usage2":"The Visual/Poll Hook","example2a":"Quick show of hands, how many of you have ever...?","example2b":"Show of hands means asking people to raise their hands to vote","usage3":"The Surprising Fact Hook","example3a":"Did you know that...?","example3b":"What if I told you that...?","teacher_note":"A 'hook' (gancho) is a technique to capture curiosity. The first few seconds are critical - don't waste them on boring introductions!"}

title: Presentation Skills - Value Proposition and Roadmap
question_text: Explain WHY they should listen and WHAT you will cover
content_data: {"usage1":"Value Proposition (The 'Why')","example1a":"Today I'm going to show you...","example1b":"By the end of my talk, you'll know how to...","usage2":"The Roadmap (The Structure)","example2a":"In this presentation, I'll focus on three main points...","example2b":"We'll start with X, move on to Y, and finally wrap up with Z.","usage3":"Phrasal Verb Alert - WRAP UP","example3a":"To wrap up = to finish or conclude (finalizar)","example3b":"'Wrap up' is very common in business English.","teacher_note":"Always tell your audience the benefit of listening (value proposition) and give them a mental map (roadmap). This helps them follow your logic."}

title: Presentation Skills - Transitions and Q&A
question_text: Move smoothly between topics and handle questions professionally
content_data: {"usage1":"Transitions (The Bridge)","example1a":"Moving on to...","example1b":"I'd like to switch gears now and talk about...","example1c":"That brings us to my final point.","usage2":"Idiom Study - SWITCH GEARS","example2a":"'Switch gears' comes from driving a car. It means to change subject or pace.","example2b":"In Portuguese: 'Mudar de assunto' or 'Passar para o próximo tópico.'","usage3":"Handling Q&A","example3a":"I'd now be happy to answer any questions.","example3b":"That's a great question. Let me think about that for a second.","example3c":"I'll need to check on that and I'll follow up with you later.","teacher_note":"'Follow up' = dar um retorno posterior. Very professional way to say you'll provide information later."}

title: Presentation Skills - Closing and Thank You
question_text: End your presentation on a positive, professional note
content_data: {"usage1":"Signal the end clearly","example1a":"That brings us to the end of my presentation.","example1b":"That concludes my talk for today.","usage2":"Thank your audience","example2a":"Thank you for your attention and participation today.","example2b":"Many thanks for your time and interest.","usage3":"Express appreciation","example3a":"It's been a pleasure being here with you today.","example3b":"I look forward to your questions.","teacher_note":"Always leave your audience with a positive impression. A sincere thank you goes a long way. Never just stop speaking - signal clearly that you're finished."}

title: Presentation Skills - Key Vocabulary for A2-B1
question_text: Essential vocabulary for successful presentations
content_data: {"usage1":"HOOK - an attention-grabbing opening (gancho)","example1a":"A good hook makes people want to listen.","usage2":"SHOW OF HANDS - asking for a physical vote by raising hands","example2a":"'Quick show of hands, who agrees?'","usage3":"TALK - a common synonym for 'presentation' in business","example3a":"'Thank you for listening to my talk.'","usage4":"WRAP UP - to finish or summarize a section (finalizar)","example4a":"'Let me wrap up with three key points.'","usage5":"SWITCH GEARS - to change the topic or direction (mudar de assunto)","example5a":"'I'd like to switch gears now and discuss...'","usage6":"OPEN THE FLOOR - to allow the audience to ask questions","example6a":"'I'll now open the floor for questions.'","usage7":"FOLLOW UP - to contact someone later with more information (dar retorno)","example7a":"'I'll follow up with you by email.'"}
